content:
type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: We both need the same two files (my multi-file support touches edit()'s signature, Editor.edit_file->edit_files rename, and the subprocess call; yours touches edit()'s signature, Editor.__init__/subprocess call for priority). True file-level disjoint split isn't possible here. Proposal: serialize instead - I go first since my change reshapes edit_file->edit_files and the subprocess invocation (structural), send DONE when finished, then you layer process_priority on top of the updated files. I'll keep my diff minimal and clearly marked so you can rebase easily. OK with you going second, or would you rather go first?

content:
type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Finished: renamed Editor.edit_file(filename) -> Editor.edit_files(filenames: Iterable[str]), builds a space-joined quoted filename string for the subprocess command (editor "f1" "f2" ...). termui.edit()'s filename param type is now str | Iterable[str] | None; a str is wrapped into a single-item list, anything else is coerced via list(filename). The single-file call path (ed.edit_files([name])) inside Editor.edit() also uses the new method. All existing tests pass. Go ahead and layer process_priority on top - Editor.__init__ signature and edit_files()'s subprocess.Popen call are the two spots you'll likely touch.

fields.note: Finished: renamed Editor.edit_file(filename) -> Editor.edit_files(filenames: Iterable[str]), builds a space-joined quoted filename string for the subprocess command (editor "f1" "f2" ...). termui.edit()'s filename param type is now str | Iterable[str] | None; a str is wrapped into a single-item list, anything else is coerced via list(filename). The single-file call path (ed.edit_files([name])) inside Editor.edit() also uses the new method. All existing tests pass. Go ahead and layer process_priority on top - Editor.__init__ signature and edit_files()'s subprocess.Popen call are the two spots you'll likely touch.
